brief: Write a paragraph about why the best ideas come when you're not trying
sample: The best ideas come when you're not trying, which is another way of saying they come when you have stopped performing the act of thinking and have instead allowed the mind to return to its own rhythms, the way one allows a horse to find its way home in the dark. We know this and yet we resist it, scheduling our brainstorming sessions and our creative hours as if illumination could be summoned by Outlook calendar, as if insight respected office hours in Midtown or Silver Lake. I have noticed that my own ideas arrive in the shower, or on the freeway between the San Bernardino exit and the merge toward LAX, or in the specific quality of boredom that descends at a dinner party in Brentwood when someone is explaining their position on interest rates—which is to say they arrive precisely when I have abandoned the notion that I am the kind of person who has ideas worth having. The mechanism seems to be this: we crowd the conscious mind with intention, with the performance of being someone who thinks, and in doing so we obscure the deeper frequencies, the ones that have been working all along in the white noise beneath our awareness, the ones that surface only when we stop listening for them.
